feedback: The model demonstrates a severe and consistent over-selection pattern across all 18 questions, achieving zero exact matches. In every single question, the model selected more options than the gold answer set, typically selecting all or nearly all available options regardless of the actual threat_level 2 (malicious) filtering required by the questions. The core failure is an inability to discriminate between signatures rated at different threat levels: the model appears to include options corresponding to threat_level 0 (informative) and threat_level 1 (suspicious) signatures alongside the genuinely malicious (threat_level 2) ones. This represents a fundamental evidence-grounding failure — the model cannot or does not apply the threat_level filter that every question explicitly requires. The Jaccard scores are moderate (averaging around 0.36) only because the gold answers are a subset of the predicted answers in most cases (recall is high but precision is very low). The model never under-selects; it always over-selects. This pattern holds uniformly across all attack families (infostealers, killers, ransomware, remcos, um_unhooking), all topics (Behavioral Analysis, Process Injection Detection), and all difficulty levels (all Hard). The model has no demonstrated ability to correctly identify the minimal set of malicious-level evidence. Training should focus heavily on: (1) threat-level discrimination tasks where the model must filter by a specific severity tier; (2) questions that penalize over-selection explicitly; (3) tasks requiring the model to cite specific signature identifiers and their threat_level fields from the detonation report before selecting options. The model should practice identifying which signatures are informative/suspicious vs. malicious and learn that selecting all plausible options is incorrect. Priority training areas: threat_level filtering, Behavioral Analysis with explicit severity constraints, Process Injection Detection with evidence grounding, and all attack families since the failure is uniform.
strengths: Zero parse failures: the model always produces a valid list of option letters in the required format.
High recall: the model almost always includes the correct gold answers within its predicted set, meaning it does identify relevant signals but cannot filter them.
Consistent response structure across all question types and attack families.

weaknesses.description: The model systematically fails to filter options by threat_level 2 (malicious). It selects options corresponding to informative (threat_level 0) and suspicious (threat_level 1) signatures alongside genuinely malicious ones, indicating it cannot apply the severity constraint that all questions require.

The model selects on average ~6 options per question when the gold set averages ~3. It never under-selects. This suggests the model defaults to selecting all plausible-sounding options rather than identifying the minimal correct set.

The model does not ground its selections in specific detonation report fields (e.g., threat_level field of each signature). It appears to reason from general malware knowledge rather than from the specific structured evidence in the report.
All Behavioral Analysis questions are answered incorrectly with over-selection. The model cannot identify which behaviors are exclusively supported by malicious-level signatures.

All Process Injection Detection questions are answered incorrectly with over-selection. The model cannot distinguish which injection-related API calls or signatures are rated malicious vs. suspicious/informative.
All 18 questions are Hard difficulty and all are answered incorrectly. The model has no demonstrated capability on hard questions requiring precise threat_level filtering.
All 3 infostealer questions answered incorrectly with over-selection.
All 4 killer/EDR questions answered incorrectly with over-selection.
All 5 ransomware questions answered incorrectly with over-selection.
All 4 Remcos RAT questions answered incorrectly with over-selection.
All 3 UM unhooking questions answered incorrectly with over-selection.
